course_description:
This course provides hands-on, advanced training for veterinary technologists. Students will be based in approved, referral veterinary practices or other animal/technical environments (e.g. zoos, research institutes, government agencies). They will work as part of a para-veterinary team in managing clinical or professional caseload and activities, under the guidance of UQ staff and an external preceptor. This Work Integrated Learning approach to training will prepare the student for an advanced level of professional practice, as well as enhancing their employment prospects through acquisition of contextually appropriate skills and competences.

Students commencing in Semester 1 full-time enrol in VETS6621 for 2 consecutive semesters. Students commencing in Semester 2 full-time enrol in VETS6622 for 2 consecutive semesters.

This course offers an individually designed program that enables students to gain hands-on experience in the practical application of a veterinary-related discipline in a work environment. Some examples of disciplines include: Anaesthesia, Surgery, Radiography, Oncology, Emergency and Critical Care medicine. At successful completion of this course, the student will be able to demonstrate advanced knowledge and mastery of skills relevant to the discipline.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:

course_aims: The aims of VETS6622 are for students to:

learning_outcomes.description: Work at an advanced level of efficiency and effectiveness in a veterinary or veterinary-related environment.
Successfully integrate and demonstrate advanced knowledge and skills in the chosen discipline to the satisfaction of the invigilator/mentor/supervisor.
Develop an understanding of industry expectations and opportunities available in relation to skills and knowledge attained during the Practicum.
Demonstrate understanding of ethical responsibilities associated with professional practice in the workplace.
Effectively apply information and compile case/project/other reports accurately and professionally.
Apply reflective techniques towards developing a practice of continual professional self-improvement.

assessment_details.title: Work Integrated Learning Placement Performance
assessment_details.due_date: Submitted by industry partner at the end of each placement
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This involves TWENTY (20) weeks clinical/industry practical placement. This equates to 100 days. Students will work with the course coordinator to design a suitable placement plan. The minimum days/week will be 3, unless a variation is approved by the course coordinator. The student must submit a calendar of days attended on placement which will be signed by the placements supervisor. The course coordinator will work with the student and practicum supervisors to ensure placement timings are designed for maximum learning opportunities. Depending on the placement and discipline, you may be required to participate in an on-call or after- hours roster. Assessment The performance will be based on four (4) key categories: Knowledge, Technical skills, Clinical skills (or discipline specific skills), and Communication Professionalism. This performance assessment will be determined following feedback from the preceptors working with you on placement. Each category has five potential responses based on the aptitude and abilities demonstrated during the rotations. Each category MUST be passed (i.e. a score of SATISFACTORY or higher) for the placement to pass the performance assessment, otherwise the performance assessment must be re-assessed following a period of re-training and/or further participation in the placement to the satisfaction of the course coordinator. Further failure will result in an overall failure for that placement. If, at this time, the student is still assessed as unsatisfactory, they will fail the course. Attendance Attendance during placement is compulsory. This is a vital part of your training and is being constantly assessed. Failure to attend one or more days should be followed by an official confirmation of the reason (e.g. medical certificate). It is then up to the student to make up this time when possible. Students can seek permission to not attend specific days of a placement from the course coordinators if for a valid reason. However, these missed days must also be completed at some other stage before a final assessment will be provided for the placement. It is the student's responsibility to organise repeat attempts with the placement supervisors and course coordinators. Staff cannot guarantee that time can be made for repeat attempts during the academic year to accommodate remediating students. Although every effort will be made to keep the student on schedule to graduate in their cohort year, repeat activities may need to be scheduled in the following semester. If a student fails a placement it is at the discretion of the placement supervisor and the course coordinator as to what remedial steps need to be taken. Students should seek feedback from the placement coordinator or course coordinator they work with throughout their placement. Attendance requirements Attendance is required and forms part of the professionalism criteria on the Performance Assessment task for each placement. Students are expected to remain until all cases or responsibilities are up-to-date and handed over/finalised in a safe and acceptable manner, and to take appropriate breaks as required. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
At the end of the placement, UQ will send a request via email to your industry partner supervisor to complete an assessment on your performance in the workplace. Supervisors will submit this report directly to UQ as per instructions in the email. The formal feedback will be released to you following review by UQ.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If all required days of the Clinical Extramural WIL Placement cannot be completed in the scheduled time due to unforeseen circumstances, the student must request an extension via my.UQ My Requests to arrange for the placement to be rescheduled by the Placements Team. Note that it may not be possible to reschedule the placement prior to the end of year results release, in which case completion of the BVetTech(Hons) program will be delayed.
Late submission
SVS Placements Team will follow up with industry partners to retrieve this assessment. Students do not need to take any action.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.late_submission: SVS Placements Team will follow up with industry partners to retrieve this assessment. Students do not need to take any action.

assessment_details.title: Mastery of Advanced Skills
assessment_details.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Performance, Placement, Practical/ Demonstration
assessment_details.other_conditions: Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The student must provide evidence of mastery of the advanced skills in the chosen discipline. A competency conversation will be held with the student and supervisor and/or the course coordinator on completion of the skills book before submission. The skills must be signed off by your supervisor and submitted to the Course coordinator by the due date. Submission may be requested as hard copy or to be entered by the student via Blackboard. Mastery of advanced skills must be clearly demonstrated in the case logs (refer to Assessment Item 3). Case log numbers (1-40), must be referenced in the skills list to demonstrate case involvement. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. assessment_details.title: Caselog
assessment_details.due_date: Case Log is due on the Placement End Date (as recorded in InPlace)
assessment_details.task_description: To pass the caselog requirement each student must upload 2 (TWO) acceptable caselogs per 5 days of placement as requested by the course coordinator. This will be via Blackboard. These caselogs will be numbered 1 - 40 and be cross referenced in the skills list. Cases selected should reflect management of patients/experiences/situations in the specified discipline . Cases selected should demonstrate your mastery of advanced skills and knowledge . Every effort to document a wide variety of advanced skills should be reflected in your case selection. You are encouraged to select cases that demonstrate more than one advanced skill. Emphasis should be on quality rather than quantity, although a sufficient caseload must be available to provide experience with all common types of cases. It is recognised that the veterinary technician has little control over quantity exposure to appropriate cases, yet the applicant is encouraged to demonstrate as much diversity as reasonably possible. Drug names (generic), dosage (mg/kg) and amount administered (in mg, not ml) should be listed whenever appropriate. For example, it is not acceptable to write “induced anaesthesia”. The actual names, dosages and amounts of pre-anesthetic and anesthetic drugs should be listed. Example of an acceptable clinical caselog below: Patient ID: 24765 Date: 10/04/17 Species: Green Tree Python (Morelia viridis) Weight: 76g Sex: Unknown Age: - Reasons for Presentation and Diagnosis: Regurgitation, hard mass in stomach area Diagnosis: Constipation Summary of treatment plan: This patient was presented to the hospital for regurgitation. Physical exam of this patient revealed a 1 cm mass, reasonably firm, caudal to its coelom, which was consistent with its left kidney. To further confirm diagnosis, an ultrasound was carried out on this patient. Ultrasound revealed a vascularized hyperechoic mass in the caudal coelom. An exploratory surgery was carried out in this patient. Prior to premedication, the patient’s vitals were taken and recorded as followed: HR 70, RR 4 and temperature: 23.8. Patient was premedicated with Hydromorphone 0.5mg/kg (0.02ml) via intramuscular injection (IM). It was then induced with Alfaxalone 10mg/kg (0.38ml) via IM and was intubated with a size 24G catheter. Throughout the procedure, patient was maintained at 0.5% oxygen due to its size and was connected to a ventilator. Patient was monitored with a capnograph, a Doppler and an ECG. The enterectomy revealed a faecal impaction, which was successfully removed. It seemed like there was potentially a partial GIT wall perforation from the hard segment. The patient had an uneventful recovery post surgery and was given the following medications: 1. Tramadol 10mg/kg 0.02ml IM q24 hours 2. Ceftazidime 20mg/kg 0.02ml IM q72 hours Patient outcome: Patient did well post surgery and was sent home after a night of monitoring with the following medications for the next 10 days. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. assessment_details.title: Assessment Sequence: Clinical Principles Questions
assessment_details.task_description: Each student will be assigned a series of THREE clinical questions relevant to their chosen discipline which they will need to research and formulate answers and submit via TurnItIn. These questions will be formulated by the Academic Supervisor in each discipline and approved by the Course Co-ordinator. Each question will be worth 15%. Each response to a clinical principles question should be 1200 - 1500 words. The word limit does not include references, tables, figures and appendices. You must include a word count on your title page. Referencing must be accurate and appropriate throughout. There should be evidence that the relevant literature has been critically analysed and thoughtfully integrated into the clinical question answer. The dates for submission will be: Question 1: 30 October 2025. Question 2: 30 January 2026. Question 3: 30 April 2026. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit your Assessment through the course Turnitin submission point in Blackboard.
Before submitting your assessment item to Turnitin, please name your file in the following way:
For example, SMITH Bob_54329876_VETS1234_Essay1
All students must ensure they receive their Turnitin receipt on submission of any assessments. A valid Turnitin receipt will be the only evidence accepted if one or more of your submissions are missing. Without evidence, the assessment will receive the standard late penalty or, after seven days, will receive zero. In the case of a Blackboard outage, please contact the Course Coordinator as soon as possible to confirm the outage with ITS.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Students with acceptable reasons (medical or compassionate reasons or as a result of exceptional circumstances) may apply for an extension of the deadline. Information on applying for an extension can be found here: my.UQ Applying for an extension .
Extension applications must be received by the assessment due date and time.

assessment_details.title: Clinical Case Report
assessment_details.task_description: A case report is a detailed report of the symptoms, signs, diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up of an individual patient. Consider some of the following ways of demonstrating your knowledge and experience in your case reports: Show how your observations, physical examination, history taking assisted the veterinarian with diagnosis. Explain why an observation was important or why you asked a certain question while taking the history. Describe the procedures you performed or assisted with. Explain why the procedure was performed. Describe how you assisted the veterinarian in treating the patient. Explain how you helped determine whether the patients treatment was being effective. Explain how your observations and monitoring helped the veterinarian modify the patient's treatment. Explain your role in planning the patient's care. Discuss your knowledge of the disease process and how it related to your nursing care, ie. what were the potential complications or risk factors, how did you recognize them, what action did you take. The case report must be no longer than 3,000 words. The word limit does not include references, tables, figures and appendices. You must include a word count on your title page. It is encouraged to submit these reports for publication. This will be a different case to the case presentation. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. assessment_details.title: Case Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: Negotiated at the start of Semester 1, 2026 in agreeance with both student and course coordinator.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Each student will be scheduled to give a 30 minute presentation. This presentation will take the form of a case report. This case report is to be drawn from cases seen during placement. Presentations can be in the form of Powerpoint, Prezi or any utilised presentation program. Guidelines Unique and interesting case management scenarios are encouraged for presentation. There is no limitation to the species or specific scenario, except that the emphasis on the will be on the chosen discipline of the student and their involvement in the case. Introduction (including the unique or interesting aspect of the case). Synopsis of the case should follow a standard outline where applicable. The following example is representative of a clinical case presentation (this would vary for non-clinical placement): Signalment Presenting complaint Pathophysiology relevant to disease process Triage or Initial Assessment Vital signs, Physical findings, Resuscitation, Stabilisation or Intervention Patient care, and recovery phase: therapeutics, monitoring Final Outcome Discussion: Should centre on specific nursing care and interventions as they pertain to the case being presented. Research must be evident from various literature sources. Conclusion: Should also include nursing key points learned from case, specials skills and/or specialised monitoring equipment utilised during the case management. Presentations should be created using PowerPoint or Prezi and brought on a memory stick. If you wish to take photographs to support your presentation, you must seek permission from the practice you are attending and the patient details must be de-identified for confidentiality. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
Students must be available for their examinations on the scheduled date, time, and campus of their course enrolment. The exam format is determined by the Course Coordinator and may include written (paper-based, online, or digital), practical, oral, or other timed assessments (such as quizzes).
If you are unable to sit for your original examination, you may be eligible for a deferred examination. To apply, you must demonstrate that unavoidable circumstances prevented you from attending your exam. If this is not possible, you can apply for a one-off discretionary deferred exam. All requests for deferred examinations MUST include supporting documentation. For more information, including acceptable evidence and application instructions, please visit my.UQ Deferring an Exam .
Please note that there are no provisions to defer an already-deferred exam. You must be available to sit your deferred examination at the allocated time.
Students who submit five or more deferral requests within a twelve-month period will be contacted with advice on the services and support available within the University.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam .

learning_activities.topic: Clinical Principles Questions Each student will be assigned a series of THREE clinical questions relevant to their chosen discipline which they will need to research and formulate answers and submit via TurnItIn. These questions will be formulated by the Academic Supervisor in each discipline and approved by the Course Co-ordinator. Learning outcomes: L05, L06

Caselogs Cases selected should reflect management of patients/experiences/other in the specified discipline. Cases selected should demonstrate mastery of advanced knowledge and skills. Every effort to document a wide variety of advanced knowledge and skills should be reflected in the students case selection. Students are encouraged to select cases that demonstrate more than one advanced skill. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Case Presentation Each student will be scheduled to give a 30 minute oral presentation. This presentation will take the form of a case report. This case report is to be drawn from cases seen during the practicum placement. This will be a different case to the written case report. Learning outcomes: L04, L05, L06

Mastery of Skills Successfully demonstrate the discipline specific clinical skills from the supplied list. Consideration will be made if there has been a lack of opportunity recognised by the preceptors. The list may be audited during the course by varying preceptors to ensure that students are actively engaged and are working through the list. It is expected that students are routinely performing the listed skills and having them signed off. Students will be counselled if they appear to making unacceptably slow progress. The students should have easy access to the paper copy of this list at all times whilst on placement in VETS6622. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05, L06

Written Case Report Students are required to submit a written case report on a case they have been involved with during their practicum. This case report is to follow the author guidelines for The Australian Veterinary Practitioner (see learning Resources on Blackboard). This will be a different case to the case presentation. Learning outcomes: L05, L06

Practicum The practicum placement is designed to expand skills and experiences in the student's particular area of interest. Students will complete the equivalent of 20 weeks of full-time placement over 2 semesters and work with highly qualified staff who are specialised in the chosen discipline. A minimum weekly commitment will be 3 days unless otherwise permitted in consultation with the Course Coordinator. Knowledge and skills building activities and learning pathways undertaken within each elected placement venue will be tailored to suit the chosen discipline. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L06

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . You'll also need to be aware of the following policies and procedures while completing this course: Fitness to Practise Policy Vaccinations and Immunisation Procedure Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy Learning activities
